claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First question: How old is the child (or children) you're inquiring about?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** typically don't qualify

Please tell me the age(s) of the child(ren) you want to claim Child Benefit for.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh hi, thanks for helping me with this! I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 17 and still living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're asking about your son Alex, who is 17 years old and living at home with you.

Since Alex is 17 (which falls in the 16-19 age category), I need to check if he meets the education or training requirements for Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to know about Alex's current education or training status.

**Is Alex currently:**
1. **In approved education or training** (such as school, college, A-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, etc.)?
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks)?
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave?

Which of these applies to Alex's situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's studying for his A-levels at the local sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, he's in approved education. Now I need to check that the level and type of education meets the requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! A-levels are definitely approved (non-advanced) education. Now I need to verify a few more details about Alex's education:

**Questions about Alex's A-level studies:**

1. Is this **full-time education** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?
2. Is this education **NOT funded by an employer** or part of an agreement for a future job with an employer?

Most sixth form A-level courses are full-time and not employer-funded, but I need to confirm this for Alex's situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's full-time - he's there Monday to Friday during the day. And no, it's not funded by any employer, he's just doing his A-levels like most kids his age.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex's A-level studies meet all the requirements - they're approved non-advanced education, full-time (12+ hours per week), and not employer-funded.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's work situation.

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week** (or not in paid work at all)?

This is important because children aged 16+ who work 24 hours or more per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they're in education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, he does work quite a bit - he has a part-time job at the supermarket and works pretty much full-time hours there alongside his studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I need to clarify this because it's important for Alex's eligibility. 

**Does Alex work 24 hours per week or more at the supermarket?**

This is the key threshold - if he works:
- **Less than 24 hours per week**: He can still qualify for Child Benefit
- **24 hours or more per week**: He would not be eligible for Child Benefit, even though he's studying

You mentioned he works "pretty much full-time hours" - could you tell me approximately how many hours per week he works at the supermarket?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he works about 24 hours a week or more - it's quite a lot alongside his studies, but he wanted to save up some money.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I understand - that's very commendable that Alex is working hard to save money while studying! However, this does affect his Child Benefit eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Result

**Result: NOT ELIGIBLE**

I'm sorry to inform you that you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

### Why Alex doesn't qualify:

Although Alex is 17 and studying A-levels (which is approved education), he is working **24 or more hours per week** at the supermarket. Under UK Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they're also in approved education or training.

### Important Information:

**If Alex's work situation changes:**
- If Alex reduces his working hours to **less than 24 hours per week** while continuing his A-level studies, he would become eligible again, and you could reclaim Child Benefit
- You should notify HMRC if his circumstances change

**What happens next:**
- If you're currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex, you'll need to inform HMRC that he's now working 24+ hours per week
- Child Benefit payments for Alex should stop

I understand this may be disappointing news, especially since Alex is working so hard balancing his studies and work. If you have any questions or if Alex's work hours change in the future, please don't hesitate to reach out.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is enrolled in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally qualify him for Child Benefit for 16-19 year olds. However, Alex is working 24 or more hours per week at a supermarket. Under UK Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they are also in approved education or training. Therefore, Alex does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 17), who is studying A-levels full-time but also works 24+ hours per week. The work hours threshold disqualifies Alex from Child Benefit eligibility despite being in approved education.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: Child aged 16+ is working 24 or more hours per week and not in approved education or training
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops if child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24+ hours per week and is no longer in approved education or training. If they return to education/training, you may be able to claim again.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and is enrolled in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally qualify him for Child Benefit for 16-19 year olds. However, Alex is working 24 or more hours per week at a supermarket. Under UK Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they are also in approved education or training. Therefore, Alex does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 17), who is studying A-levels full-time but also works 24+ hours per week. The work hours threshold disqualifies Alex from Child Benefit eligibility despite being in approved education.
